visual_checks: Look for text rendered as images instead of live HTML text — common in logos, banners, and headings
Check headings: are they rendered as or background images containing text instead of HTML text?
Look for buttons that are images containing text instead of styled HTML buttons
Check for infographics or banners where the text is baked into the image
Look for navigation items that are image-based rather than text-based
Check promotional banners and hero sections for images of text
pass_conditions: Text is rendered as actual HTML text rather than images of text
CSS styling is used to achieve the desired visual presentation of text
Where images of text are used, they are customizable by the user (e.g., server-side text rendering that accepts user style preferences)
Images of text are only used for logotypes/brand names where the particular visual presentation is essential
Charts and graphs that contain text have that text also available in accessible form (data table, long description)
fail_conditions: Page headings rendered as images containing text instead of styled HTML headings
Navigation buttons or links implemented as images of text
Banner or hero text that is part of an image rather than overlaid HTML text
Informational content rendered as images of text that could be presented as HTML with CSS
Call-to-action buttons implemented as images with text baked in
Body content rendered as image scans of text documents
na_conditions: No images of text are present on the page
All images of text are logotypes or brand names where the specific presentation is essential
The particular presentation of the text IS essential (e.g., a font sample, historical document reproduction)
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
Cannot distinguish between images of text and styled HTML text from DOM alone without visual inspection
Image content could not be loaded to determine if it contains text
common_mistakes: Using image headers instead of web fonts with CSS styling for custom typography
Embedding text in promotional banners instead of overlaying HTML text on a background image
Scanned PDF content displayed as images instead of accessible HTML or tagged PDF
Navigation menus using image sprites with text baked into the images
Using image-based buttons because they were easier to style than CSS buttons

examples.pass: The site uses Google Fonts and CSS to style headings with a custom typeface — all text is live HTML that can be selected, copied, and resized by the user
examples.fail: The page header 'Welcome to Our Company' is an tag containing the text in a decorative font, rather than using a web font with CSS — the text cannot be resized, recolored, or read by screen readers without alt text
examples.partial: Most page text is HTML but the promotional banner uses an image containing the sale price and description text that could have been rendered as HTML over a background image

plain_meaning: When the technology in use can render real text with the needed visual style, authors should use real text instead of pictures of text. Images of text cannot be resized cleanly, cannot be restyled by users, and lose fidelity at high zoom. Logotypes and cases where a specific visual presentation is essential are exempt.
scope_applies_to: Headings, banners, and hero text rendered as images
Button labels rendered as image files
Menu items or navigation links rendered as image graphics
Promotional graphics containing informational text baked into the image
Quotes, announcements, or marketing copy composited into images
scope_does_not_apply_to: Text where a particular visual presentation is essential to meaning (e.g., a font sample, a signature)
Decorative imagery with no informational text
Elements removed from the accessibility tree (aria-hidden, display:none)
Visually-hidden / sr-only alternatives that pair with an image of text (they belong to SC 1.1.1)
Text rendered via web fonts or SVG <text> elements (that is real text, not an image of text)
Captions in video, which are a different SC
borderline_calibration: SVG that uses <text> elements is REAL text, not an image of text. Do not flag.
Web fonts rendering real text characters are REAL text. Do not flag.
A logo containing the brand name is exempt regardless of style.
An image of text used alongside an equivalent real-text version is still a technical failure but severity should be lower.
Hero banners that bake tagline text into a JPEG are a classic failure when the technology clearly supports real text.
